coredata.dc:title: Facile hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen, phosphorus-doped fluorescent carbon dots for live/dead bacterial differentiation, cell imaging and two nitrophenols detection
coredata.dc:creator: Tu, Yujiao
Wang, Suping
Yuan, Xiaotian
Xiang, Yingying
Qin, Kunhao
Wei, Yunlin
Zhang, Qi
Chen, Xuemei
Ji, Xiuling
coredata.dc:description: The blue fluorescent nitrogen, phosphorus doped carbon dots (Tg N,P-CDs) deriving from Thelephora ganbajun Zang were successfully obtained by hydrothermal method. Tg N,P-CDs possessed excellent fluorescence features and displayed strong blue fluorescence emission at 444 nm under 356 excitation with good water solubility. Tg N,P-CDs exhibited low biotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility by live/dead bacterial differentiation, cell imaging and onion epidermal staining. Furthermore, the nanoprobes showed high selectivity and sensitivity in detecting 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP) and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), and excellent linear range of 0–50 μM (R2 = 0.9923) and 0–50 μM (R2 = 0.9926) with low detection limits of 35.09 nM and 78.62 nM for 2,4-DNP and 4-NP, respectively. The Tg N,P-CDs was successfully applied to determine 4-NP and 2,4-DNP in real water and soil samples. It revealed that the inner filter effect quenching mechanism dominated in quenching of Tg N,P-CDs fluorescent by 2,4-DNP and 4-NP. It provides a new insight on the bioimaging application and environmental pollutant detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Thelephora ganbajun Zang
Live/dead bacterial differentiation
Bioimaging
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of T. ganbajun Zang-derived CDs
Characterization of the prepared Tg N,P-CDs
Quantum yield measurement and fluorescence lifetime measurements
Cytotoxicity evaluation and cell imaging of Tg N,P-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a) (insert: HR-TEM), size distribution (b) and FTIR spectrum (c) of Tg N,P-CDs.
XRD pattern (a) and XPS full scan spectrum (b) of the prepared Tg N,P-CDs.
UV–Vis, excitation and emission spectra (a) and Emission of Tg N,P-CDs (b) at various excitation wavelengths.
Live and dead bacteria staining with the prepared Tg N,P-CDs at bright field and fluorescence images by CLSM.
CLSM images of HT29, U2OS and 293 T cells with Tg N,P-CDs.
CLSM images of onion epidermal cells with Tg N,P-CDs.
Fluorescence quenching efficiency of aromatic compounds (a) and metal ions (b) to Tg N,P-CDs.

Fluorescence spectra of Tg N,P-CDs with various concentrations of 2,4-DNP (0–100 μM) (a) and 4-NP (0–250 μM) (b). Relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0–F)/F0 of Tg N,P-CDs and 2,4-DNP (0–100 μM) (c) and 4-NP (0–250 μM) (d) concentration. Linear relationship between (F0–F)/F0 of Tg N,P-CDs and 2,4-DNP (0–50 μM) (e) and 4-NP (0–50 μM) (f) concentration.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of Tg N,P-CDs, 2,4-DNP, and Tg N,P-CDs +2,4-DNP (a), Tg N,P-CDs, 4-NP, and Tg N,P-CDs +4-NP (b); the FL decay lifetime of Tg N,P-CDs and Tg N,P-CDs +2,4-DNP (c), Tg N,P-CDs and Tg N,P-CDs +4-NP (d); UV–Vis absorption of 2,4-DNP (e), 4-NP (f) and fluorescence spectra of the prepared Tg N,P-CDs.
Comparison of LOD with the reported methods of 4-NP determination.
Analytical results of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP in the water samples by Tg N,P-CDs (n = 3).
Analytical results of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP in the soil samples by Tg N,P-CDs (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Facile hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen, phosphorus-doped fluorescent carbon dots for live/dead bacterial differentiation, cell imaging and two nitrophenols detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Science and Technology, Yunnan Key Laboratory of Food-Safety Testing Technology, Kunming University, Kunming, 650214, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Science and Technology
Yunnan Key Laboratory of Food-Safety Testing Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Science and Technology, Kunming University, Kunming, 650214, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650500, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Stomatology, Yan′an Hospital Affiliated to Kunming Medical University, Kunming, 650031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yan′an Hospital Affiliated to Kunming Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Stomatology, Yan′an Hospital Affiliated to Kunming Medical University, Kunming, 650031, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors have contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The blue fluorescent nitrogen, phosphorus doped carbon dots (Tg N,P-CDs) deriving from Thelephora ganbajun Zang were successfully obtained by hydrothermal method. Tg N,P-CDs possessed excellent fluorescence features and displayed strong blue fluorescence emission at 444 nm under 356 excitation with good water solubility. Tg N,P-CDs exhibited low biotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility by live/dead bacterial differentiation, cell imaging and onion epidermal staining. Furthermore, the nanoprobes showed high selectivity and sensitivity in detecting 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP) and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), and excellent linear range of 0–50 μM (R2 = 0.9923) and 0–50 μM (R2 = 0.9926) with low detection limits of 35.09 nM and 78.62 nM for 2,4-DNP and 4-NP, respectively. The Tg N,P-CDs was successfully applied to determine 4-NP and 2,4-DNP in real water and soil samples. It revealed that the inner filter effect quenching mechanism dominated in quenching of Tg N,P-CDs fluorescent by 2,4-DNP and 4-NP. It provides a new insight on the bioimaging application and environmental pollutant detection.

Schematic of the synthetic route for fluorescent Tg N,P-CDs and the applications in live/dead microbial differentiation, cell imaging, 2,4-DNP and 4-NP detection.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution has become more and more serious. The continuous release of many artificial chemicals not only affects the quality of the environment, but also poses a threat to human health [1]. Phenolic pollution mainly is the pollution of phenolic compounds to water bodies [2]. Phenolic wastewater is one of the main sources of water pollution. Phenol is contained in wastewater discharged from many industrial fields such as coking, refining, metallurgy, petrochemical and chemical organic synthesis [3–5]. If these wastewater is directly discharged without treatment, then soil and food will be contaminated. 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP) is an important chemical intermediate in sulphur picric acids, wood preservatives and dyes [6–8], and also used as a preservative, and bioaccumulation pesticide. 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) is one of the most toxic pollutants. The sources of 4-NP include the manufacture of leather, medicine, pesticide, dye and anti-fungal agent [9]. Thus, due to their toxicity and carcinogenicity, 2,4-DNP and 4-NP were enlisted in the “Priority Pollutant List” by U. S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [10].

As a new kind of nano materials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous interest and been applicated in detecting environmental pollutant due to easy preparation, high fluorescence intensity, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility properties [11–15].

Currently, more and more research on the preparation of CDs from biomass has been reported [16–20], but relatively few from macroedible fungi substrates. Macroedible fungus contains rich protein and amino acid, especially lysine, vitamin and a variety of mineral element. Thelephora ganbajun Zang, belong to Basidiomycota, Agaricomycetes, Thelephorales, Thelephoraceae, Thelephora. It is wild edible and medical fungi, and contains 19.2% crude protein, 7.9% crude fat, 16.1% ash and 15.8% total sugar, also contains a variety of amino acids that the body cannot synthesize. There are high nutritional value and powerful health function in Thelephora ganbajun Zang. It showed the functions of anti-oxidation, anti-tumor and improving immunity. In this study, green non-toxic fungus biomass raw materials T. ganbajun Zang, diammonium hydrogen phosphate and ethanediamine were employed to prepare water-soluble Tg N,P-CDs via one-step hydrothermal method. The properties and applications of Tg N,P-CDs deriving from T. ganbajun Zang are systematically performed. Tg N,P-CDs play multifunctional roles in live/dead bacterial differentiation, cell imaging, 2,4-DNP and 4-NP detection. To our knowledge, this is the first report on green non-toxic fungus biomass raw materials T. ganbajun Zang-derived fluorescent nanoprobe for two nitrophenols pollutant detection. It showed good prospects in live/dead bacterial differentiation and cell imaging.

All analytical reagents, including4-chlorophenol (4-CP), nonylphenol (NP), m-aminophenol (MAP), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), phenol (P), p-aminophenol (PAP), 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP), 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP), o-aminophenol (OAP), 2,4,6-trichlorophenol (2,4,6-TCP), CuSO4, CrCl6, MgSO4, CsCl, CrCl3, Ni(NO3)2, HgCl2, Pb(NO3)2, BeCl2, CdCl2, MnSO4, CoCl2 and AlCl3 were provided by Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).
Luria-Bertani (LB) liquid media (10 g/L peptone, 5 g/L yeast extract, and 10 g/L NaCl, pH 7.0) was employed to culture Escherichia coli BW25113, Staphylococcus aureus and Bacillus cereus MYB41-22.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of T. ganbajun Zang-derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Tg N,P-CDs from T. ganbajun Zang were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. Typically, 4 g T. ganbajun Zang, 1 g (NH₄)₂HPO₄ and 2 mL ethanediamine (EDA) were mixed and then dissolved in 50 mL sterile water, and transferred to 100 mL high-pressure autoclave for reaction at 200 °C for 12 h. The product was cooled naturally, centrifuged (13,000 g, 20 min), filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane and then stored (4 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the prepared Tg N,P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The characterization of the prepared Tg N,P-CDs was investigated. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F30S, FEI, Netherlands) was used to obtain TEM images of Tg N,P-CDs. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (TENSOR 27, Bruker, Germany) was employed to determine the surface chemistry characteristics of Tg N,P-CDs. XPS spectra (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. U.S.A.) was used to determine the binding energy of electrons to achieve qualitative analysis of surface elements. The crystalline phases were measured by XRD on a Bruker D8 Advance. Fluorescence emission spectrum (G9800A fluorospectrophotometer, Agilent Technologies, USA) and UV–Vis absorption spectrum (UV-2600 spectrophotometer, Shimadzu, Japan) were used to determine the optical properties of Tg N,P-CDs. Spectrofluorometer (FS5, England) was used to determinate the lifetime. The zeta average potential was determined by Zeta potential analyser (Microtrac Inc.).

Three type bacteria were used as the target of differentiation with Tg N,P-CDs, i.e., E. coli BW25113 belonging to Gram-negative bacteria, while B. cereus 41-22 and S. aureus, belonging to Gram-positive bacteria. Three type bacteria were incubated in LB liquid media at 37 °C for 12 h. Dead cells were obtained by treatment with 2% formaldehyde solution for 2 h, in the meantime, the live cells treated with phosphate buffer solution as control. Three kinds of live or dead bacterial cells (109 cfu/mL) were mixed with 500 μg/mL Tg N,P-CDs (37 °C, 2 h, 150 rpm). After centrifugation (10,000 g, 2 min), the supernatant was discarded. The precipitate was washed three times with sterile water to remove the excess Tg N,P-CDs. Then all bacterial cells were suspended with 500 μL sterile water and imaged under a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). The cytotoxic effects of Tg N,P-CDs (0–1000 μg/mL) towards bacterial cells were checked by plate count method.

The cell toxicity of the prepared Tg N,P-CDs was assayed with U2OS, HT29 and 293 T cells by 3-(4,5)-dimethylthiahiazo (-z-y1)-3,5-di-phenytetrazoliumromide (MTT) method [19,21]. Sterilized cell slide was put into a 24-well plate, then U2OS, HT29 and 293 T cells were cultured at 37 °C for 24 h with 5% CO2, then the cells were serum-starved for 1 h, and washed with phosphate buffer solution after the media was removed. Tg N,P-CDs with different concentrations were added and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h with 5% CO2. All samples were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 30 min, and the cell imaging was performed on CLSM.

The innermost layer of onion was cut into 2 cm × 2 cm small pieces, soaked in the carbon solution for 2.5 h, and then was washed with sterile water until no color present. The inner layer of the onion was removed and placed on the slide, then cell imaging was performed on CLSM.

Typically, the prepared Tg N,P-CDs (2 μL) solution and phosphate buffer solution (998 μL) were mixed, different concentrations of 2,4-DNP or 4-NP solution were added, and then diluted to 4.0 mL with deionized water. The fluorescent intensity was measured on the fluorescence spectrophotometer after 3 min at the slit width of 2.5 nm. The selectivity of 2,4-DNP or 4-NP and other substances were investigated to study the coexisting interfering substances on the PL intensity, such as P, 4-CP, NP, PAP, 4-NP, 2,4-DCP, OAP, 2,4-DNP, MAP, 2,4,6-TCP, Cu(II), Mn(II), Cr(III), Pb(II), Cs(I), Cr(VI), Hg(II), Al(III), Co(II), Cd(II), Be(II), Mg(II), and Ni(II).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield measurement and fluorescence lifetime measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield (QY) was determined according to the classic operating procedures. The QY of quinine sulfate dissolved in H2SO4 (0.1 M) was 54% at excited wavelength 360 nm, and assigned as a reference [12]. The following equation was applied:
Q
CDs = Q
R (I
CDs /I
R ) (A
R /A
CDs ) (η
CDs
2/η
R
2)
Where the subscript CDs refers to prepared Tg N,P-CDs and R refers to reference; Q
CDs refers to QY of the prepared fluorescence Tg N,P-CDs, I is the integrated emission intensity, η is the solvent refractive index (η
R = 1.33), and A refers to the absorbance value at the excitation wavelength.

Average lifetime model is as follows:
τ =
∑
A n
τ n 2
A n
τ n
( n = 1,2,3 … … )

The fitting model is E-exponential model:
Y= B + A1*exp(-i/τ1) + … + An × exp(-i/τn) (n = 1,2,3 …)

To study the practicality of the prepared fluorescence Tg N,P-CDs sensor system, the same experiment was performed in tap, lake, waste water and soil samples with and without 2,4-DNP and 4-NP, respectively. The lake and waste water samples were obtained from laoyu lake and dye factory of Kunming city, Yunnan Province. The water samples were collected and centrifuged (13,000 g, 15 min) and then filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane in order to remove impurities.

The morphology of Tg N,P-CDs was observed by TEM (Fig. 1 a). We can find that the Tg N,P-CDs have uniform spherical particles (1.6–7.5 nm), and the average diameter is about 4.40 nm (Fig. 1b). FTIR spectroscopy was used to determine the surface chemistry characteristics (Fig. 1c). A strong absorption peak at 3456 cm−1and 1541 cm−1 displays the presence of O–H/N–H and CC/N–H bonds [21]; the peak at 1481 cm−1 indicates the OC–H/C–N bonds, and the peak at 801 cm−1 denotes C–H/O–H bonds [22].

The XRD pattern exhibited good amorphous nature with a broad peak at 2θ = 22.57° (Fig. 2 a). The five peaks at 290.8 eV, 529.2 eV, 398.8 eV, 184.7 eV and 133.4 eV are presented by the full scan XPS spectra, attributed to C1s, O1s, N1s, S2p and P2p, respectively (Fig. 2b) and the peaks from C1s spectrum at 284.2 eV, 285.3 eV and 287.2 eV, corresponding to C–C, C–N and CO [23], respectively (Fig. S1a). Two peaks appear at 398.2 eV and 399.8 eV from N1s spectrum, assigned to C–N–C and N–C [23], respectively (Fig. S1b) and two peaks from O1s spectrum at 530.3 eV and 531.8 eV, attributed to CO and C–O/C–O–C (Fig. S1c) [23]. The results of the XPS diagram are consistent with that of the FTIR spectrum, indicating the prepared Tg N,P-CDs having abundant hydrophilic and amino functional groups on the surface, and possessing good water solubility [19].

The optical properties of the prepared Tg N,P-CDs was determined with fluorescence emission spectrum and UV–Vis absorption spectrum [21]. Tg N,P-CDs shows a strong absorption peak around 280 nm, attributed to the π-π* transitions (CC bond), and a weak absorption peak around 366 nm, attributed to the n-π* transitions (CO bond) (Fig. 3 a) [19,21,24]. The maximum emission wavelength is 444 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength (356 nm) (Fig. 3a). When the excitation wavelength shifts from 280 to 350 nm, the PL intensity enhances, and when it shifts from 360 to 390 nm, the PL intensity weakens, and meantime, the emission peak exhibits red-shift, indicating good excitation-dependent character of Tg N,P-CDs (Fig. 3b).

The fluorescence stability of the Tg N,P-CDs was determined under different salt concentrations and pH values. The PL intensity only fluctuates slightly without significant change when the NaCl solution concentration increases (0–1.0 mM) (Fig. S2a), indicating good fluorescence stability [21]. With the pH buffer changing from 2.2 to 5.0, the fluorescence intensity increases, and then decreases at 6.0–12.0 (Fig. S2b), which demonstrates that the optimal pH is 5.0. Tg N,P-CDs solution was storaged for 10 months, and the fluorescence intensity value was measured and recorded every month to investigate the storage stability (Fig. S2c). With the increase of storage time, the fluorescence intensity value did not change significantly within 9 months. It showed that Tg N,P-CDs solution had strong anti-bleaching ability and good optical stability.

The quantum yields (QY) of the Tg N,P-CDs was determined to be 9.44% (Q
R = 0.54, I
R = 671.536, I
CDs = 301.749, A
CDs = 0.036, A
R = 0.014) based on standard quinine sulfate (QY = 54%), while that of Tg CDs (only Thelephora ganbajun Zang as material) was 7.25% (Q
R = 0.54, I
R = 671.536, I
CDs = 90.129, A
CDs = 0.014, A
R = 0.014).

The prepared Tg N,P-CDs were used for live bacterial staining, but we found that the live bacteria could not be labeled by the fluorescent multicolor probe Tg N,P-CDs (Fig. 4 ). Then the dead bacterial staining was performed. E. coli BW25113, B. cereus 41-22 and S. aureus were treated with 2% formaldehyde solution for 2 h to obtain dead bacterial cells. It showed that all the three types dead bacterial cells could be obviously stained by Tg N,P-CDs (50 μg/mL) and observed by a CLSM in blue, green and red colours (Fig. 4), indicating that the Tg N,P-CDs is a good candidate for of live/dead bacterial differentiation and bioimaging [20].

We can find that the prepared Tg N,P-CDs can successfully differentiate live/dead bacteria. It is generally considered that electrostatic interaction between bacterial cells and CDs, and the selective permeability of bacterial cells towards CDs are the main keys for live/dead cell differentiation [20]. The zeta potential values of live E. coli BW25113, B. cereus 41-22 and S. aureus are −43.63 mV, −40.00 mV and −30.50 mV, respectively. The zeta potential values of Tg N,P-CDs is −29.25 mV. Thus, live bacterial cells cannot be stained by Tg N,P-CDs due to the stronger electrostatic repulsion between live bacterial cells and Tg N,P-CDs (Fig. 4). The zeta potential values of dead E. coli BW25113, B. cereus 41-22 and S. aureus were measured to be −23.07 mV, −22.67 mV and −19.03 mV, respectively, after treatment with 2% formaldehyde solution for 2 h, indicating that electrostatic interaction between Tg N,P-CDs surface and dead bacterial cells is obviously weakened. Bacterial cell membranes possess selective permeability, and small molecular substances entering cells usually require transportation by ion channels or transmembrane proteins, thus live bacterial cells cannot be labeled. However, the cell walls or membranes of the dead bacteria were destroyed and the ability of selective permeability deduced, thus the dead cells could be successfully labeled by Tg N,P-CDs.
Plate count method showed that bacterial viability had no significant decrease, even when the Tg N,P-CDs concentration reached 1000 μg/mL, manifesting the low cytotoxicity of Tg N,P-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity evaluation and cell imaging of Tg N,P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the bioimaging application potential, the cytotoxicity of the prepared Tg N,P-CDs was measured by MTT method [29]. The viability of the U2OS, HT29 and 293 T cells cultivated with Tg N,P-CDs was measured, and untreated cells were used as control. We can find that cell viability showed no significant decrease, even when the Tg N,P-CDs concentration reached 2000 μg/mL (Fig. S3), indicating a low cytotoxicity of the prepared Tg N,P-CDs.

The multicolor PL and good biocompatibility demostrated that the prepared Tg N,P-CDs were suitable for bioimaging applications, and then in vivo cell imaging was carried out by CLSM. After incubation with Tg N,P-CDs (50 μg/mL, 37 °C, 2 h), the U2OS, HT29 and 293 T cells emitted different fluorescence in the cytoplasm and were obviously labled by Tg N,P-CDs. All three channels (green, blue and red channels) at 405, 488 and 543 nm excitation wavelengths, respectively, were clearly observed, which manifested that the Tg N,P-CDs could be effectively internalized (Fig. 5 ). Furthermore, we did not observe the substantial morphological changes in HT29, U2OS and 293 T cells, which further verified the low cytotoxicity.

In order to study the biodistribution of Tg N,P-CDs in plant, onion epidermal cell staining was performed. As shown in Fig. 6 , green, blue and red channels were clearly observed, and no obvious plasmolysis was observed, so it implied that the cell membrane and wall were close together. There was a clear boundary between the onion epidermal cells which stained by Tg N,P-CDs. It may mean that the Tg N,P-CDs are attached to the cell membrane or entered into the periplasm between cells. The nucleus staining was also clearly observed. It is speculated that Tg N,P-CDs can enter onion cells, successfully attach, and then image the cell membrane and nuclear membrane. It indicated that the prepared Tg N,P-CDs can be functioned with good biocompatibility. The onion epidermal cell staining successfully broadens the application of CDs in plant imaging.

The selectivities of the Tg N,P-CDs was investigated. The fluorescence intensity of different possible interfering substances including P, 4-CP, NP, PAP, 2,4-DCP, 4-NP, MAP, 2,4,6-TCP, OAP, 2,4-DNP, Cu(II), Cs(I), Cr(VI), Hg(II), Ni(II), Pb(II), Al(III), Cd(II), Mn(II), Cr(III), Be(II), Co(II) and Mg(II) were studied. We found that 2,4-DNP and 4-NP could effectively quench the PL intensity of Tg N,P-CDs (Fig. 7 ). Therefore, high selectivity between Tg N,P-CDs and 2,4-DNP or 4-NP is existed.

The pH affects not only the CDs PL intensity, but also the quenching efficiency of quenchers on CDs. For 2,4-DNP detection, the PL intensity increases within pH 2.2–5.0 and the maximum fluorescence quenching efficiency is reached, but the PL intensity decreases after pH 5.0, thus the optimum pH value of 2,4-DNP detection is pH 5.0 (Fig. S4a). For 4-NP detection, the PL intensity increases within the pH 2.2–7.0, but the PL intensity decreases after pH 7.0, therefore, the optimum pH value of 4-NP detection is pH 7.0 (Fig. S4b).

In order to investigate the interfere with each other of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP, the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0–F)/F0 of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP towards Tg N,P-CDs were performed at pH 5.0 and pH 7.0. It showed that the two types of test objects did not interfere with each other at different pHs (Fig. S5).

The quenching mechanisms of quencher to fluorescence CDs mainly include static quenching (SQ), dynamic quenching (DQ), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE) and aggregation induced emission quenching (AIEQ) [9,35].

To verify the quenching mechanisms of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP towards Tg N,P-CDs, UV–Vis absorption spectra of Tg N,P-CDs, 2,4-DNP, 4-NP and Tg N,P-CDs with 2,4-DNP or 4-NP were determined (Fig. 9 a -9b). We can find that the absorption of the Tg N,P-CDs does not change with or without 2,4-DNP and 4-NP, which indicates that no new substance has formed. So, they do not belong to the SQ process.

As shown in Fig. 9c and d, the FL decay lifetime of Tg N,P-CDs does not change without (2.13 ns) or with 2,4-DNP (2.11 ns) and 4-NP (2.12 ns). So the quenching mechanism of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP may be IFE [36].

The absorption spectra of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP overlap with the excitation spectra of Tg N,P-CDs (Fig. 9e and f). Thus, we inferred maybe IFE dominated in quenching process. In addition, the Parker equation was used to verify the IFE mechanism [35].
F
c o r
F
o b s d
= 2 . 3 d
A
e x
×
10
g
A
e m
× 2 . 3 s
A
e m
/ ( 1 -
10
- d
A
e x
) × ( 1 -
10
- s
A
e m
)

F
cor and F
obsd are the observed PL intensity and the corrected PL intensity, respectively; A
ex and A
em are the absorbance value at the maximum excitation wavelength, and that at the maximum emission wavelength, respectively; d is the cuvette width; s refers to the excitation beam thickness; g refers to the distance between the edge of cuvette and excitation beam [35].

With 2,4-DNP concentration increasing (0–25 μM), F
cor
/F
obsd value of Tg N,P-CDs increases from 2.17 to 2.89, which meet the Parker equation. With 4-NP concentration increasing (0–25 μM), F
cor
/F
obsd value increases from 1.32 to 1.52, which also match the Parker equation. Therefore, IFE is further considered to be dominate in quenching process of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP towards the fluorescent of Tg N,P-CDs.

The blue fluorescent Tg N,P-CDs derived from T. ganbajun Zang were prepared via simple one-step hydrothermal synthesis method. This is the first report on synthesizing CDs with T. ganbajun Zang, (NH₄)₂HPO₄ and EDA as the precursor. Morphology, chemical element composition and optical properties of the prepared Tg N,P-CDs were characterized. The Tg N,P-CDs showed excellent fluorescence features and exhibited strong blue fluorescence emission at 444 nm under 356 excitation. The prepared Tg N,P-CDs could selectively differentiate live/dead bacterial cells, and possessed low biotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility and multicolor fluorescence emission properties by U2OS cell, HT29 cell, 293 T cell and onion epidermal cell imaging. In addition, 2,4-DNP and 4-NP could quench the PL intensity of the prepared Tg N,P-CDs. Excellent linear relationships were obtained between the fluorescence reaction of prepared Tg N,P-CDs and the concentrations of 2,4-DNP or 4-NP in the range 0–50 μM, with the detection limits of 35.09 nM and 78.62 nM under optimized conditions, respectively. The quenching mechanisms of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP towards Tg N,P-CDs belonged to IFE. Therefore, as the synthetic method is simple, it is an excellent candidate for bioimaging application and high sensitivity detection of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP.
Tu Yujiao, Wang Suping, Xiang Yingying: Methodology. Yuan Xiaotian, Qin Kunhao: Data curation.Chen Xuemei, Zhang Qi: Investigation, Validation. Wei Yunlin, Ji Xiuling: Writing-Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially funded by Science Research Foundation of Yunnan Provincial Department of Education of China (2020J0509) and Young Talents Support Program of Yunnan Province (Ten Thousand People Plan) (YNWR-QNBJ-2019-202).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2020.108761.
